question_text: A 58-year-old man presents with gross hematuria. CT urogram shows a 3.5 cm heterogeneously enhancing solid mass arising from the upper pole of the right kidney with enhancement of 85 HU on the nephrographic phase. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common primary renal malignancy in adults (85% of cases). Enhancement >15–20 HU on CT is the key indicator of vascularity/malignancy. Clear cell RCC typically shows marked heterogeneous enhancement. Angiomyolipoma contains fat (negative HU). Oncocytoma may appear identical but is a benign diagnosis of exclusion. Renal abscess presents with fever, positive urine culture, and rim-enhancing fluid collection.

question_text: On CT, an adrenal mass measures 8 HU on unenhanced images, 68 HU on portal venous phase, and 22 HU on 15-minute delayed images. Which calculation is most useful in characterizing this as a lipid-poor adenoma?
option_a: Absolute percentage washout (APW) = [(Enhanced HU − Delayed HU) / (Enhanced HU − Unenhanced HU)] × 100
option_b: Relative percentage washout (RPW) = [(Enhanced HU − Delayed HU) / Enhanced HU] × 100
option_c: Net enhancement = Enhanced HU − Unenhanced HU
option_d: Signal intensity index on chemical shift MRI
explanation: For a lipid-poor adenoma (unenhanced HU >10), absolute percentage washout (APW) ≥ 60% is diagnostic of adenoma. In this case: APW = [(68 − 22) / (68 − 8)] × 100 = [46/60] × 100 = 77% → adenoma. RPW ≥ 40% can also be used when unenhanced images are unavailable. Chemical shift MRI uses the signal intensity index and is the MRI equivalent approach.

question_text: A 45-year-old man presents with acute scrotal pain of 4 hours duration. Scrotal ultrasound shows absent intratesticular blood flow on Doppler and a 'whirlpool sign' of the spermatic cord. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: MRI of the scrotum for further characterization
option_c: Urology consult in 24 hours; repeat Doppler in 6 hours
explanation: This is testicular torsion — a surgical emergency. The absent Doppler flow and 'whirlpool sign' (twisting of the spermatic cord) are diagnostic. Salvage rates are >90% within 6 hours, dropping to <50% at 12 hours, and near zero at 24 hours. Immediate manual detorsion followed by urgent surgical exploration and bilateral orchiopexy is the standard of care. MRI and further imaging only delay treatment. Antibiotics would be appropriate for epididymo-orchitis.

question_text: On multiparametric MRI of the prostate, a 62-year-old man has a 1.5 cm lesion in the peripheral zone showing marked low T2 signal, early restricted diffusion with ADC 650 mm²/s, and early arterial enhancement on DCE. According to PI-RADS v2.1, what category is this lesion?
explanation: In the peripheral zone, DWI is the dominant sequence for PI-RADS scoring. Markedly low ADC with marked focal high signal on high b-value DWI = DWI score 5 → PI-RADS 5. A peripheral zone DWI score of 5 indicates a score ≥4 even before adding DCE. The combination of all three sequences (T2, DWI, DCE) all being abnormal solidifies PI-RADS 5. Positive DCE (early arterial enhancement) can upgrade a PI-RADS 3 DWI lesion to 4 in the peripheral zone. PI-RADS 5 = very high probability of clinically significant cancer (≥PPV 88%).

question_text: A 72-year-old woman is found to have a right adrenal mass incidentally. On CT, the mass measures 2.5 cm and contains areas of macroscopic fat (−30 HU). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Macroscopic fat within an adrenal mass is virtually pathognomonic of myelolipoma. Myelolipomas contain hematopoietic marrow elements and mature fat, resulting in areas of HU values ranging from −30 to −100. They are benign, non-functioning tumors requiring no treatment unless large (>4-5 cm) due to risk of hemorrhage. Adrenal metastases show no fat. Pheochromocytoma can occasionally contain small foci of fat but is predominantly soft tissue. ACC is a large, heterogeneous, aggressive mass.

question_text: A 35-year-old woman with tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) has multiple bilateral renal lesions containing macroscopic fat. The largest measures 5.5 cm. She presents with acute flank pain and CT shows a perirenal hematoma. What is the most likely diagnosis and complication?
option_a: Renal cell carcinoma with spontaneous rupture
option_b: Angiomyolipoma with Wunderlich syndrome (spontaneous retroperitoneal hemorrhage)
explanation: Angiomyolipoma (AML) is strongly associated with TSC (80% of TSC patients have AML). The hallmark is macroscopic fat on CT (HU < -10). Lesions >4 cm carry increased risk of spontaneous hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome — spontaneous non-traumatic retroperitoneal hemorrhage), likely due to aneurysm formation within abnormal vessels. This is the most common serious complication. Prophylactic embolization or ablation is recommended for AMLs >3-4 cm or those growing rapidly.
reference: Flum AS et al. J Urol 2016;196:1345-1352

question_text: A 55-year-old man undergoes CT for flank pain. A renal cyst is seen with a thin septa, few thin internal septations, and no enhancement. It measures 4 cm. According to the Bosniak classification, what category is this cyst, and what is the recommended management?
option_c: Bosniak IIF — follow-up imaging in 6 months, then annually for 5 years
option_d: Bosniak III — surgical resection or ablation
explanation: A cyst >3 cm with a thin septa qualifies as Bosniak IIF (the F denotes 'follow-up required') under the 2019 Bosniak classification update. Bosniak IIF cysts have a ~5-10% malignancy rate. Recommended surveillance: 6-month CT/MRI, then annual for 4 more years (total 5 years). If stable, no further follow-up. Bosniak I = simple cyst (no follow-up). Bosniak II = minimally complex (no follow-up). Bosniak III and IV warrant surgical or ablative management.

question_text: A 65-year-old man with a history of diabetes presents with fever and right flank pain. CT shows streaky perirenal fat stranding, parenchymal gas, and loss of normal renal architecture on the right. Blood cultures are positive for E. coli. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Emphysematous pyelonephritis (EPN) is a life-threatening necrotizing infection with gas production within the renal parenchyma, collecting system, or perinephric space. It is almost exclusively seen in diabetics (90% of cases). CT is the gold standard for diagnosis — demonstrating intraparenchymal gas, parenchymal destruction, and perirenal involvement. Huang and Tseng classification guides management. Class 1-2: antibiotics + drainage. Class 3-4: nephrectomy often required. Mortality rate is 20-40% despite treatment.
reference: Huang JJ, Tseng CC. Arch Intern Med 2000;160:797-805

question_text: A 28-year-old man undergoes fluoroscopic retrograde urethrogram after a pelvic fracture. Contrast extravasates superior to the urogenital diaphragm into the periprostatic and perivesical space. The bladder neck appears intact. What type of urethral injury does this represent?
option_b: Type I posterior urethral injury — stretching without tear
option_c: Type II posterior urethral injury — tear at prostatomembranous junction above urogenital diaphragm
option_d: Type III posterior urethral injury — tear extending through urogenital diaphragm into bulbar urethra
explanation: The posterior urethra is the most commonly injured segment in pelvic fractures. The Goldman classification describes three types of posterior injuries. Type I: stretching/elongation without tear (contrast fills urethra normally). Type II: tear at the prostatomembranous junction — contrast extravasates above the urogenital diaphragm (periprostatic/perivesical space). Type III: complete disruption through the urogenital diaphragm — contrast extravasates both above and below it. Type II injury is suggested here by contrast above the diaphragm with intact bladder neck.
reference: Goldman SM et al. J Urol 1997;157:85-89

question_text: A 40-year-old woman presents with cyclical pelvic pain and infertility. Pelvic MRI shows bilateral adnexal cysts with T1 high signal that does NOT suppress on fat saturation, and T2 low signal 'shading.' What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Endometriomas (chocolate cysts) are the classic T1 bright, T2 dark ('T2 shading') lesion on MRI. Key distinction from dermoid: T1 high signal that does NOT suppress on fat saturation (because the high T1 signal is from blood/hemosiderin, not fat). Dermoid cysts show T1 high signal that DOES suppress on fat saturation. T2 shading is caused by recurrent hemorrhage with concentration of blood breakdown products. Bilateral adnexal endometriomas in a woman with cyclical pain and infertility = classic presentation.

question_text: On ultrasound, a 25-year-old male has a hypoechoic intratesticular mass. Tumor markers show elevated AFP and beta-hCG. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Non-seminomatous germ cell tumors (NSGCTs) — particularly embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, and choriocarcinoma — are associated with elevated AFP and/or beta-hCG. Pure seminomas may have mildly elevated beta-hCG (syncytiotrophoblastic cells) but do NOT produce AFP — elevated AFP always indicates a non-seminomatous component. NSGCTs tend to be more heterogeneous, with cystic, hemorrhagic, and calcific components. Leydig cell tumors produce androgens/estrogens. Epidermoid cysts are benign with a characteristic 'onion-skin' appearance.

question_text: A 30-year-old woman in the 8th week of pregnancy presents with acute pelvic pain and vaginal bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a 2 cm tubal ring sign in the right adnexa with free pelvic fluid. Serum beta-hCG is 2,500 mIU/mL. What is the next most appropriate management step?
option_a: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours; repeat ultrasound if rising
option_b: MRI of the pelvis for characterization
option_c: Immediate gynecology referral for ectopic pregnancy management (methotrexate or surgery)
explanation: The 'tubal ring sign' — an echogenic ring surrounding a gestational sac-like structure in the adnexa — with an empty uterus and free pelvic fluid is diagnostic of a tubal ectopic pregnancy. The discriminatory zone for beta-hCG is 1,500-2,000 mIU/mL — at this level, an IUP should be visible by transvaginal US. Empty uterus above the discriminatory zone = high suspicion for ectopic. Free fluid indicates possible rupture. This requires urgent gynecologic evaluation. Methotrexate is used for stable unruptured ectopic; surgical management if hemodynamically unstable or ruptured.

question_text: A 55-year-old man undergoes CT cystogram after motor vehicle accident. Contrast injected retrograde through a Foley catheter extravasates into the paracolic gutters and between bowel loops. No contrast extravasates into the perivesical fat. What type of bladder injury is this?
option_d: Combined intra- and extraperitoneal bladder rupture
explanation: Intraperitoneal bladder rupture occurs when blunt trauma causes the dome of the bladder (the only peritoneum-covered portion) to rupture, allowing urine to freely enter the peritoneal cavity. CT cystogram findings: contrast in the paracolic gutters, between bowel loops, and around the liver/spleen. Treatment: surgical repair. Extraperitoneal rupture (most common, associated with pelvic fractures) shows contrast limited to the perivesical fat and pelvis — 'starburst' or 'molar tooth' pattern. Key teaching: ALWAYS fill the bladder with 350 mL of contrast for adequate CT cystogram.

question_text: MRI of the pelvis for cervical carcinoma staging shows a 4.2 cm cervical mass with disruption of the low T2 signal cervical stroma and extension into the parametrium, but the pelvic sidewall is not involved and there is no hydronephrosis. What is the FIGO stage?
explanation: FIGO 2018 staging: Stage I = confined to cervix. Stage IIA = upper 2/3 vaginal extension, no parametrial involvement. Stage IIB = parametrial involvement WITHOUT pelvic sidewall involvement. Stage IIIA = lower 1/3 vaginal involvement. Stage IIIB = pelvic sidewall involvement OR hydronephrosis/non-functioning kidney. Stage IVA = bladder/rectal invasion. This case shows parametrial involvement with no sidewall disease = FIGO IIB. MRI is the modality of choice for local staging — disruption of the low T2 cervical stromal ring is key for diagnosing parametrial invasion.
reference: Bhatla N et al. Int J Gynaecol Obstet 2018;143:129-135

question_text: A 68-year-old man with a history of smoking has a CT KUB showing a 1.5 cm filling defect in the mid-ureter with proximal hydronephrosis. Ureteroscopy shows a papillary lesion. Biopsy confirms urothelial carcinoma. Which surveillance protocol is most appropriate after definitive treatment?
option_a: No follow-up needed if resection margins are negative
option_c: Cystoscopy every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months, plus urine cytology; CT urogram annually
option_d: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast at 6 months only
explanation: Upper tract urothelial carcinoma (UTUC) has a high risk of synchronous (10-20%) and metachronous (30-50%) bladder urothelial carcinoma. Surveillance after treatment includes cystoscopy every 3 months for 2 years (then every 6 months) due to this risk, plus urine cytology. CT urogram annually assesses the contralateral upper tract and bladder. Risk factors include Lynch syndrome, aristolochic acid exposure, high-grade tumors, and multifocal disease — these may warrant more intensive surveillance.
reference: Rouprêt M et al. Eur Urol 2021;79:62-79

question_text: A 50-year-old woman undergoes pelvic MRI for abnormal uterine bleeding. The uterus appears globular with diffuse thickening of the junctional zone measuring up to 18 mm and multiple small T2 hypointense foci within the myometrium. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Adenomyosis is characterized on MRI by: (1) junctional zone (JZ) thickening ≥12 mm (normal JZ ≤ 8 mm; 8-12 mm equivocal), (2) globular uterine configuration, and (3) T2 hypointense myometrial foci or T1/T2 bright foci (ectopic endometrial islands and hemorrhage). The junctional zone is the inner myometrium visible on T2 as a low-signal band. The threshold of ≥12 mm JZ thickness is the most reproducible diagnostic criterion. Fibroids are discrete masses with well-defined borders and a pseudocapsule, causing mass effect rather than diffuse infiltration.
reference: Reinhold C et al. Radiographics 1999;19 Spec No:S147-60

question_text: On non-contrast CT of the abdomen, a left adrenal mass measures 35 HU and is 4.5 cm. On enhanced CT, it measures 110 HU at 60 seconds and 75 HU at 15 minutes. The APW is 32% and RPW is 32%. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: No further workup; this is a benign adenoma
option_b: Adrenal protocol MRI with chemical shift sequences
explanation: APW <60% and RPW <40% with a unenhanced HU >10 means the mass CANNOT be characterized as a lipid-poor adenoma by CT washout criteria alone. Next step is chemical shift MRI — in-phase and opposed-phase sequences. Lipid-rich adenomas show a drop in signal intensity (SI) on opposed-phase images (SI index >20% drop). If MRI is also indeterminate, FDG-PET can help distinguish benign from malignant — adenomas are typically FDG-negative, malignant masses are FDG-avid. CT-guided biopsy is reserved for cases with known primary malignancy where confirming adrenal metastasis will change management.

question_text: A 42-year-old man with hypertension and episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis undergoes CT. An adrenal mass is found measuring 5 cm with heterogeneous enhancement and central necrosis. 24-hour urine metanephrines are elevated. What diagnosis does this imaging pattern suggest?
explanation: Pheochromocytoma is the classic 'rule of 10s' tumor: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal (paraganglioma), 10% malignant, 10% in children. The episodic triad of hypertension, headache, and diaphoresis with elevated urine metanephrines is classic. CT shows a heterogeneous, avidly enhancing mass with central necrosis ('light bulb' enhancement on MRI with marked T2 hyperintensity — 'salt and pepper' sign on MRI). CRITICAL: NEVER biopsy an adrenal mass without first ruling out pheochromocytoma — hypertensive crisis can be fatal. Biochemical diagnosis must precede imaging-guided biopsy.
reference: Pacak K et al. Ann Intern Med 2001;134:315-329

question_text: A 62-year-old man has a horseshoe kidney found incidentally. Which of the following complications is MOST associated with horseshoe kidney?
option_a: Increased risk of renal cell carcinoma at a rate 2x the general population
option_b: Ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) obstruction due to anomalous crossing vessels
explanation: Horseshoe kidney is the most common renal fusion anomaly (1:400). The isthmus fuses the lower poles anterior to the aorta and IVC, preventing normal ascent. Key associations: (1) UPJ obstruction — most common complication, due to anomalous/aberrant vessels crossing the UPJ anteriorly and high insertion of ureter, leading to hydronephrosis; (2) nephrolithiasis (50% of patients); (3) pyelonephritis; (4) increased risk of transitional cell carcinoma and Wilms tumor (not RCC specifically). UPJ obstruction affects ~30% of patients and may require dismembered pyeloplasty.
reference: Natsis K et al. Folia Morphol 2014;73:1-13

question_text: A 55-year-old man with aortic aneurysm repair 3 years ago presents with bilateral hydronephrosis and renal insufficiency. CT shows soft tissue density surrounding the periaortic region, encasing the ureters and causing medial deviation of the ureters. No lymphadenopathy is seen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Retroperitoneal fibrosis (RPF) is characterized by a fibro-inflammatory rind of tissue encasing the aorta and IVC, causing medial deviation and obstruction of the ureters (unlike lymphoma/sarcoma which displace ureters laterally). RPF can be idiopathic (Ormond disease), secondary to drugs (methysergide, beta-blockers), malignancy (desmoplastic metastases), or post-surgical. CT shows periaortic soft tissue that may enhance acutely. Medial ureteral deviation is the classic finding — in lymphoma, ureters are displaced laterally by enlarged nodes.

question_text: A 75-year-old woman undergoes CT for hematuria. A sessile filling defect is identified in the bladder on CT urogram, measuring 3 cm with irregular margins and early arterial phase enhancement. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: Repeat CT in 3 months to assess stability
option_d: MRI of the bladder for further characterization
explanation: A sessile, enhancing bladder mass with irregular margins in an elderly patient with hematuria is highly suspicious for urothelial carcinoma of the bladder. CT urogram can identify the lesion but cannot provide tissue diagnosis. Cystoscopy with biopsy is the gold standard — it allows direct visualization, biopsy for histology, and assessment of the degree of muscle invasion (TURBT). MRI is used for STAGING known bladder cancer (T2 = muscle invasion), not for initial diagnosis. CT is excellent for detecting lesions ≥5 mm but tissue diagnosis is always required.
reference: Wolff EM et al. J Urol 2009;182:28-35

question_text: Radiopaedia case: A 64-year-old man with elevated PSA. Multiparametric MRI of the prostate is performed. The case is presented with T2, DWI, and DCE sequences. A 12mm lesion in the right peripheral zone shows marked T2 hypointensity with markedly restricted diffusion (ADC ~600 mm²/s) and positive DCE. Which PI-RADS v2.1 score best describes this lesion?
explanation: In PI-RADS v2.1, for the peripheral zone, DWI is the dominant sequence. A lesion ≥15mm with markedly restricted diffusion scores DWI 5. Any peripheral zone DWI score of 5 = overall PI-RADS 5, regardless of T2 or DCE scores. For lesions <15mm: DWI 4 + positive DCE → upgrades to PI-RADS 4. DWI 3 + positive DCE → upgrades to PI-RADS 4. A 12mm lesion with ADC ~600 and markedly restricted diffusion = DWI score 4/5. Given positive DCE, the overall score reaches PI-RADS 5. PI-RADS 5 carries >60-80% risk of clinically significant prostate cancer (Gleason ≥3+4).
reference: Turkbey B et al. Eur Urol 2019;76:340-351

question_text: A 45-year-old woman presents with acute right flank pain. CT without contrast shows a 6mm calcification at the right ureterovesical junction with moderate right hydroureteronephrosis. The stone density is 800 HU. Which stone type is most commonly associated with this HU range, and what does HU of 800 predict about spontaneous passage?
option_a: Uric acid stone — high likelihood of spontaneous passage
option_b: Calcium oxalate/phosphate — high density predicts harder stone, lower spontaneous passage rate
explanation: Calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate stones (most common overall, ~80%) are the densest on CT, typically >600 HU. Higher HU correlates with harder stones and lower spontaneous passage rates, but the primary predictor of spontaneous passage is stone SIZE and LOCATION. Stones ≤4mm at the UVJ pass spontaneously in >80% of cases. 6mm stones at UVJ: ~50-60% pass with medical expulsive therapy (alpha-blockers). Uric acid stones are characteristically radiolucent on plain X-ray (50-200 HU on CT) and respond to urine alkalinization. Struvite stones are 'staghorn calculi' associated with urease-splitting organisms.
reference: Türk C et al. Eur Urol 2022;82:1-47

question_text: A 38-year-old woman with known fibroids presents for MRI prior to uterine fibroid embolization (UFE). One fibroid measures 7cm and shows complete T2 hypointensity (very low signal). Another 4cm fibroid shows markedly T2 hyperintense signal. What does this T2 signal difference predict about UFE response?
option_a: Both fibroids will respond equally well to UFE
option_b: The T2 hypointense fibroid will respond better; T2 hyperintense (cellular) fibroids have lower response rates
option_c: T2 hyperintense fibroids respond better due to higher vascularity
option_d: T2 signal has no predictive value for UFE response
explanation: T2 signal intensity of fibroids predicts UFE response. Classic (hypocellular, fibrous) fibroids show low T2 signal and have excellent UFE response rates (80-90% volume reduction). T2 hyperintense (cellular) leiomyomas have higher water content, more cells, and less fibrous stroma — they are more likely to have residual perfusion after embolization and have lower volume reduction rates. The FIBROID Registry and multiple studies show T2 signal is a significant predictor. Conversely, degenerated fibroids may show mixed T2 signal. Pre-UFE MRI is used to map fibroid number, location, size, and T2 characteristics for counseling and planning.
